1: shadow Praise is due to you, O God, in Zion; and to you shall vows be performed. The praise due in Zion is the praise of the church gathered around the risen Christ. He is the one who performs the ultimate vow — the vow of the Servant who gives himself for the many (Isaiah 53:12). Through him all vows to God are fulfilled in the Yes of Christ (2 Corinthians 1:20).
2: direct O you who hear prayer, to you shall all flesh come. Jesus applies this universal coming to himself: And I, when I am lifted up from the earth, will draw all people to myself (John 12:32). The one to whom all flesh comes is the crucified and risen Christ — the fulfillment of this verse's promise that prayer reaches the one who gathers all humanity.
3: shadow When iniquities prevail against me, you atone for our transgressions. The atonement — you atone for our transgressions — is God's own act, accomplished through Christ: God was in Christ reconciling the world to himself, not counting their trespasses against them (2 Corinthians 5:19). Iniquity prevails against Christ on the cross; God atones through that very event.
4: shadow Blessed is the one you choose and bring near, to dwell in your courts! The chosen one brought near is Christ — the elect Servant (Isaiah 42:1; 1 Peter 2:4): he chose us in him before the foundation of the world (Ephesians 1:4). Through Christ, believers too are brought near to dwell in God's courts (Ephesians 2:13 — brought near by the blood of Christ).
5: shadow By awesome deeds you answer us with righteousness, O God of our salvation, the hope of all the ends of the earth and of the farthest seas; The hope of all the ends of the earth — you will receive power when the Holy Spirit has come upon you, and you will be my witnesses... to the end of the earth (Acts 1:8). Christ is the hope (Colossians 1:27) brought to the farthest seas by the missionary church empowered by the Spirit.
6: revelation of God the one who by his strength established the mountains, being girded with might; The creator God whose might established the mountains is the one who, in Christ, says I have overcome the world (John 16:33). The same power that set the mountains in place raised Jesus from the dead — Paul identifies it as the same divine strength (Ephesians 1:19-20).
7: shadow who stills the roaring of the seas, the roaring of their waves, the tumult of the peoples, Christ stills the storm: he rebuked the wind and said to the sea, Peace! Be still! And the wind ceased, and there was a great calm (Mark 4:39). The disciples asked, Who then is this? — the creator God who stills the seas is present in human flesh.
8: shadow so that those who dwell at the ends of the earth are in awe at your signs. You make the shouts of the morning and the evening ring out with joy. The signs of Christ — healings, resurrections, the cross and empty tomb — inspire awe at the ends of the earth (Acts 4:16 — a notable sign has been performed, and we cannot deny it). The shouts of the morning ring out at the resurrection announcement.
9: shadow You visit the earth and water it; you greatly enrich it; the river of God is full of water. Christ the living water visits the earth in the Incarnation. The river of God full of water is the Spirit poured out: out of his heart will flow rivers of living water (John 7:38-39 — he said this about the Spirit). The enrichment of the earth by Christ's coming and the Spirit's outpouring is the new covenant's abundance.
10: shadow You water its furrows abundantly, settling its ridges, softening it with showers, and blessing its growth. The parable of the sower (Matthew 13:1-23) — Christ the sower watering the earth with the word, seeking fruitful ground. The rain that softens soil is the word and Spirit working together to produce the harvest of the kingdom.
11: shadow You crown the year with your bounty; your wagon tracks overflow with abundance. The abundance of the messianic age: the wedding at Cana (John 2:1-11 — 180 gallons of finest wine), the feeding of the 5,000 (twelve baskets left over), the feeding of the 4,000. Christ's presence crowns every moment with bounty — the overflowing abundance of the kingdom.
12: shadow The pastures of the wilderness overflow, the hills gird themselves with joy; The wilderness restored: Isaiah's vision of the desert rejoicing (Isaiah 35:1) finds its fulfillment in Christ who makes the wilderness blossom — healing in deserted places (Matthew 14:13-21), feeding crowds in the wilderness (Matthew 15:32-38), transformed lives spreading joy through desolate regions.
13: shadow the meadows clothe themselves with flocks, the valleys deck themselves with grain, they shout and sing together for joy. The eschatological harvest: Christ's parable of the harvest (Matthew 13:39 — the harvest is the end of the age); the reaper's joy (John 4:35-36 — the fields are white for harvest). The whole creation's singing anticipates Paul's vision: the creation itself will be set free from its bondage to corruption (Romans 8:21).
